text: Hello, I'm sharing a acute cholecystitis. This was done on call. Patient came in through the emergency room after about twenty-four hours of severe discomfort and workup confirming acute cholecystitis.

I chose to do the surgery robotically. I think that the options of laparoscopy and robotic are pretty equivalent other than perhaps surgeon preference. And recently I have just been a lot more comfortable sitting at the console rather than standing at the bedside for these more challenging or expectedly challenging gallbladders.
The surgeon removes adhesions between the omentum and the liver, along with some perinephric fat. The dissection of the gallbladder begins on its lateral side.

The surgeon uses ICG fluorescence to assist in visualizing the anatomy during the cholecystectomy. A large stone is visible lodged in the neck of the gallbladder, which complicates the manipulation and exposure of the gallbladder.

The surgeon uses scissors and a hook to manipulate the gallbladder during the cholecystectomy. The common bile duct is visible when the ICG mode is activated, and the surgeon attempts to identify and dissect the cystic duct.

The surgeon encounters challenges while dissecting the medial side of the gallbladder and decides to return to the lateral side for easier dissection and better exposure. The gallbladder is flipped back to create a window behind it, freeing the tissue up to the top of the gallbladder, facilitating the establishment of the critical view.

The surgeon attempts to create a window at the junction of the cystic duct and gallbladder, noting the close proximity of the artery running alongside the cystic duct. Manipulation of the gallbladder is challenging due to the artery's position.
The surgeon employs a top-down approach to detach the gallbladder from the liver bed, improving exposure of the cystic artery situated above the cystic duct.

The surgeon approaches the cystic artery and cystic duct complex, carefully freeing the surrounding adventitial tissue to expose the junction between the cystic duct and the common bile duct. The cystic artery is visible, and the surgeon prepares to place clips on it.
The surgeon addresses bleeding from the cystic artery on the specimen side, maintaining control while cleaning the surgical field.
The surgeon creates a window beneath the cystic artery, using focused bipolar cautery to control bleeding. With improved exposure from previous mobilization, clips are applied to secure the artery.

The surgeon thins out the remaining tissue on the cystic duct, shaping it into a funnel to facilitate clip placement. The cystic duct, initially wide, narrows as it approaches the junction with the common bile duct, allowing for appropriate clip application.

The surgeon continues thinning the tissue around the cystic duct for clip placement, achieving a clear critical view of the cystic duct, common bile duct, and common hepatic ducts. Clips are applied to the cystic duct, which is then transected.
Case overall went very well. Took just about one hour start to finish. I was very pleased. And my arms and my fingers didn't hurt when I was done. I hope you enjoyed.
